speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in New York. And a friend of mine met me. Otto Libling's his name. He met me at the-- at the-- at the port. And also a representative-- a representative from the United Jewish Appeal. And they sent me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I had no money. I came-- I arrived in this country, I had a nickel in my pocket. You know, like a bon vivant I spent it on the boat. You know? I had fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw-- went to the-- they sent us to the Hotel Marseille, up-- up in New York. Was-- we were refugee-- all refugees, you know, were sent to this particular hotel. I stayed there for about three weeks. And finally, they called me-- they called me in to downtown New-- downtown New York, to the office of the United Jewish Appeal. And they start to talking to me. You know, I figured maybe they forgot me. I don't-- I didn't want-- I didn't like New York, right from then on. You know, I figured-- this kind of a city, if I want to become an American, this is the wrong place to be. Because everybody speaks all sorts of languages. The foreigners speak English. I didn't-- Excuse me. I didn't like the hustle and bustle of New York. It's-- later on, I had a chance to go for business to New York, and I was always glad to get out. And so finally, they-- they interviewed me. Naturally, I had no-- no qualifications. I had no-- no-- no business experience or job experience-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said-- I said, but I don't want to be in New York. They said, where do you want to go? You want to go to Boston, you want to go to Pittsburgh, want to go to Philadelphia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remembered, being from Leipzig, that the Boston Symphony Orchestra was always a great orchestra. So I flipped a coin-- heads or tail. I know it was Pittsburgh or Boston. The Boston came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I want to go to Boston. I figured, it's close enough to New York, in case nothing works out, I can always run back to mama, you know? To New York. And so they-- they gave me enough money. They bought me a ticket. Gave me enough money and sent me to Boston.

speaker: interviewee
text: There I was met, again, by a representative of the-- of the United Jewish Appeal. And they put me up with a Jewish family in Brookline, Massachusetts-- which is part of-- it's part of Boston, you know. It's-- And there, two days later, I had a job in Cambridge, Mass., washing dishes in a Greek restaurant-- owned by a Greek fellow. You know, it was-- it was not a public restaurant, it was more in an industrial park. The restaurant with all-- all the people. Anyhow, after a while, I've got-- you know, I go around and started to meet some people. And there's one-- one fellow says, come to this-- there's a nice-- we have a Jewish organization, Zionist organization, called Masada. Come on, and you'll meet some-- meet some people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went-- I went to this-- this place. And I found out that they had a-- they had a Jewish-- this particular evening was a Jewish-- everybody was supposed to bring a Jewish-- something about Jewish music. OK, Naturally, I didn't have nothing. I didn't-- I was a guest there. So this one girl, she had a record from Johann Sebastian Bach. I said to myself-- I said-- I said to this young lady, you got Johann Sebastian Bach. You know, I says, Bach and I are landsmen. You know, we come from the same town. I says, I come from Leipzig.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, you know, one thing led to the other. [LAUGHS] And that-- this girl later on became my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Verna. Maiden name is [? Biurak. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married on September 2, 1950. She comes from a very nice Jewish family-- great Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Oh, yes. We are blessed with-- we have three children. Our oldest one is Dr. Carl-- my son-- very proud of it. And he's a podiatrist and lives in Houston, Texas. He's married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born October 30, 1952. Then we have-- our next one is Dr. Steven. He lives in-- he's an obstetrician, gynecologist. He lives in Phoenix, Arizona. He was born May 18, 1955. And our third one is Robbie-- which you're gonna meet later on-- he-- he was born on June 4, 1960. That was the day after-- my wife was born on June 3, 1930, and she thought maybe she should have him on the same-- the same day that he was born-- a day-- a day later. Later-- Later on, you will meet Robbie and his-- his wife, Sandra. And Steven's wife is Yael. They have two lovely girls, they live in-- Rachel and Ester Flora. They live in Phoenix, Arizona. My oldest son, Carl, married Jan [INAUDIBLE]. She's from San Antonio. They-- They only got married in March, so there's no children yet. But right now we're blessed with four grandchildren and there's two-- you'll  meet Sandra later on-- there's two-- she's-- she's carrying twins. So, please God, there's two on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to school in-- my wife, who was very, very influential in my life, my education, I went back to Brookline High at night. See, I had no papers-- nothing. I went back to Brookline High School, in Brookline, Massachusetts. Of course, and also-- she also made me take courses at Boston University evening school. OK? My wife also hired her English teacher from school to give me English lessons. She was a dental nurse at the Beth Israel Hospital. Very successful. And I owe a great deal to-- [LAUGHS] to-- to my wife, who is-- who is not here today. She's in Phoenix. But I'm very proud of her, because she-- I think, was the most influential part of my life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, you know, we both worked hard. She worked very hard to bring up the kids. I would say the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think we're successful in our children, you know? In their professional lives. And it's due to her, much more to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was-- I was working. I was always-- I was always trying to be ahead-- being-- being a foreigner, you know, in this country, always having had an accent. You know, not born in here. No-- No family, except her. And she comes from a very nice family-- very spread-out family. And so she-- she brought up the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your primary occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. After-- After I worked in this place, for washing dishes, you know, I finally made contact with the Jewish Vocational Service in Boston. And they test me, and they thought I would be good for business. So I applied for the-- At that time, Filene's Department Store, in Boston, had what was called the Filene's Executive Training School. And that was-- you had to pass an exam. I passed everything except English. I flunked English. But they made exceptions to me. You had to-- you worked-- you went to school in the morning, and then, in the afternoon, you worked in the store. You know, you learned retailing, buying-- all-- all those things that was retailing. I don't think they have the school anymore, today. But there were people from Harvard and everybody else trying to apply into this thing. And so I was-- I was with Filene's for-- for eight years. They sent me down to the famous Filene's Basement. Now, this is not to be confused with what you have in the basement. You have Filene's Basement today in all different cities. There was only one store in the whole world. It was Filene's Basement in Boston. One store. I worked in the men's department. I started off as a stock boy and eventually became assistant buyer. Later on, I transferred-- I transferred to-- to the upstairs stores. I was department manager in men's in two branches. And then, through the grapevine, I got an offer to a store to go to Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went-- So I accepted the job. I went to Chicago, and I worked for-- for two companies in Chicago. And eventually-- again, through the grapevine-- I had an offer to come back East.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, while in Chicago, our older son went to the yeshiva. Which, today, I think didn't have a good influence on him. You know? But that's beside the point.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- I came back. I came back to-- accepted a job. I left Chicago. We went back to-- to Cape Cod, Massachusetts-- Hyannis-- where we resided for the last 30 years. We just bought-- we sold this place, end of August. We lived in a house in Hyannis, Massachusetts. Robbie, who you're gonna meet, actually grew up there. All my kids went to school there. And after Filene's-- no, wait a minute. Here I'm in Chicago. I joined this company, Puritan Clothing Company, in Hyannis. Later on, I went to another store. That was the [INAUDIBLE] Company in Boston. I was a buyer there. And when that-- that place went out of business. Then the last job I had, I joined Fruit of the Loom, the famous underwear company. I was a sales representative for Southeastern Massachusetts. And I was there for the last 16 years, until 1992, when I retired. When I reached age 65, I retired. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war broke out, you were a student. Did you have any professional aspirations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- I always wanted to be a journalist, you know, like in newspapers. As a matter of fact, at one time, I-- I wrote this-- this fellow-- [? Tito ?] Morealli, who is Dr. Morealli's son, in Italy-- he and I started a little-- a little newspaper. And I wrote an editorial. And unfortunately, he lost it and I lost it. It was only in Italian. I wrote an editorial for this newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I always wanted to be a newspaper man, you know, like Walter Cronkite or-- or whatever, you know? An editorial writer for a newspaper. Whatever it is, that was my main ambition. If you had the ability to become a news journalist right now, and you would be able to write your life story, what would be the headlines, about your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my life? 'I'm a lucky survivor.' OK? Or 'God was with me, all the way.' You know, that's-- I brought my kids up in here-- our kids up here. I really didn't tell them too much about me. The first time we went to-- we went to a-- went to a wedding in Israel, I took all three children to Israel. And I took my oldest son to Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, Dad, how come you never told us-- told me-- about this thing? And I said, honestly, I didn't want the children to be burdened in their growing years. Don't forget, they went through the '60s and another thing, I did not want to burden them with the Holocaust thing. I figured there'd be times later on, when they grow older, that they'll understand it better and I'll tell them about my experience. You picked this time to tell your children, through this tape-- your story. Is there a message that you want to leave with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Continue on, Jewish-- the Jewish life. Be-- be great-- great parents. Do the best you can. I'm very proud when I see my grandchildren say the kiddush or listen or say the [INAUDIBLE]. I'm very, very proud, because I know my parents-- they must kvell, in heaven, to see this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue on-- just-- just keep [? the-- ?] being Jewish-- love for Israel, be good husbands, be good parents, be good Americans-- whatever you want to be. Be a good citizen, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And because your mother and father tried very hard to be good parents to you and instill in you the Jewish life, even though we are not-- we are not religious today. We're not Orthodox or anything else. But there's certainly a kind of Yiddishkeit, a Jewishness, in us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hope you can continue on-- carry on this tradition. Because-- how you say it? Because your grandparents died a kiddush hashem, just because they were Jews. OK? I mean, my parents-- my parents-- not-- my wife is-- my wife is an American, even though she has-- she was born here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents died just because they were Jews. And don't forget it. Always be a Jew, regardless of what it is, and be proud of it. I am always-- I am proud of being a Jew. I'm proud of being an American today. And I have a great love for Italy. Israel is-- is in my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I mean, I look at the newspaper-- the first thing I look-- is anything going on in Israel? I've been there 10, 12 times. My wife has been there many times. She's been more than often than I have. She loves Israel. So we-- we both have a great love for Israel. And let's continue on this-- this-- [INAUDIBLE] Israel, what do you call it-- the love for Israel. That's-- that's the message I can leave to our children. Is there anything else you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just thank you very much for giving me the opportunity to do this. It was unexpected. I wish my wife would have been here, to be at my side, you know, because she's-- she's-- she's part of my life. But she couldn't. She's in Phoenix right now. We're trying to buy a house in Phoenix, to be closer to the children. So it was very hard for her. This was completely unexpected. As a matter of fact, when I talked to David, I originally said, maybe we can push it into March, when we'd be closer to here. My wife would be here, too. And he-- he suggested that it's better to get this done. And we can always add things onto it. But we wanted to get this-- I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So thank you very much for giving me the opportunity to tell my story. And I hope that in years to come, people will learn from it and see that I was one of the lucky survivors. I was never touched by the Nazis. My family did not make it. But somehow God wanted-- wanted me to-- to-- to live, to tell the story. Here I am. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, the picture you're seeing here is my maternal grandfather, Reb Gershon Ucker. He is in the one-- the one in the middle. Exactly. And next to him is the famous Vizhnitzer Rebbe. This picture was taken in 1930, probably in Carlsbad, Czechoslovakia, where all these-- these Hasidim used to go for cures and everything else. I don't know who the other people in the picture. But that's my-- the Vizhnitzer Rebbe's supposed to be a distant relative of ours. Great wonder rabbi. This picture was a few kids from our group, up in [INAUDIBLE], Yugoslavia, Slovenia, and probably taken about 1941. I am the second from the left. The-- next to me, on the right, is a fellow by the name of Johnny [? Zamora, ?] who-- he was about the oldest in the group. He lives-- He lives now in the New York area. I forgot the names of the other ones. I'll have to look through the-- through the list. But these-- these was some of the people of-- from our group in Yugoslavia. We were together. OK, this picture, it's me. This was taken about 1943, in Nonantola, Italy, in the Villa Emma. I was working-- one of my jobs was being in the office-- you know, do some office work. And that's-- that's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Arnold Wininger, or Arnold 'WY-ninger.' And there I was nice and thin, thinking about what's going to happen next. What-- I was-- I always got myself a soft job. OK, this is our wedding picture. This is my lovely wife, Verna [? Biurak. ?] This picture was taken on September 2, 1950, in Brookline, Massachusetts, when we got married. And, please God, thank God, last September we celebrated 46 years of marriage. OK. This-- I call this picture, 'Out of the ashes of the Holocaust.' This picture was taken in March of 1996, in Houston, Texas, when our older son Carl got married. You see from the left is our youngest son, Robert-- Rob. And next to me-- it's me. Then in front of me is my wife, Verna. Next to it is our newest addition is Jan. And next to it is our oldest son, Dr. Carl. And next to it is our middle son, Dr. Steven. Next to my wife, you see Yael. No, next to her, you see-- I'm sorry, that's Sandra, which you can see later on. She's holding Adam in her arms. And next to her is Yael holding Ester Flora in her arms. In front, you see-- on the left, you see our granddaughter Rachel and then Eli. Rachel belongs to Dr. Steven and Yael, and Eli belongs to Rob and Sandra. Which you-- You're gonna see them later on. I'm very proud of this picture. This is the whole family together, which does not happen too often. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please introduce us to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be my pleasure! [LAUGHS] This is Adam. This is Adam Wininger. This is Eli Wininger. This is our two-- our two grandsons. This is Robert, my youngest son-- our youngest son. Robert Wininger and his lovely wife, Sandra Wininger.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to, first of all, thank them very much for their hospitality. And I'd like to thank them for organizing this whole thing. I came here-- this was a complete surprise. But Robbie and Sandra thought this was very important for the family, to have this-- this documentary of my life. So thank you very much, children. I'm very, very proud of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I was-- today we went-- we went with the children to the Jewish community center. They had Shabbat. There is real Jewish life. And I'm very happy that they continuing on the Jewish tradition of the family. And I'm very, very proud of-- of the children. So-- and before--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before I say anything else, I'd like to mention two people. They're not here today. I'd like to thank them. This is my cousins Nathan and [? Dina ?] Kurzberg in Haifa, Israel, who've been very, very influential in our lives. OK? So I'd like to say shalom, and we love you. Is there anybody else who'd like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think I'd like to say something. My dad's gonna be 70. And I just want to thank the Wiesenthal Center and whoever and Steven Spielberg-- all the people that were involved-- because I'm very excited that all my life Dad has-- he never would bring up the Shoah-- the Holocaust. But we would ask him about it, and he never withheld anything. And there's this incredible amount of history and information that he has. And-- and I just want-- I've always been telling him to write it down, to-- to-- to tape it-- do something, so I can have something to give to our children. And-- thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I know I'm going to start crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- we really have a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a beautiful present. It's my father's 70th birthday, and this is a gift from you, and from him to us. And to give to my children and children's children. And let people know what happened. It's a hell of a part of history.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish we never had to bother doing this kind of a thing. But, you know, it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: I only wish that my wife, his mother, would be here today. But for logistic reasons, she couldn't-- she couldn't be here today. But we have her picture. And she's been a great influence in all of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You guys want to say anything? Eli?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say anything to Grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Want to say 'Shabbat Shalom'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat Shalom? I had a-- I enjoyed today, when you had the Shabbat at the JCC. It was wonderful. I was very, very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very proud when you said-- you said the kiddush and you said the hamotzi. I was very, very proud of you kids, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: So shall we all say 'bye-bye'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye-bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say 'bye-bye.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye-bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you wave bye-bye, Eli? Wave bye-bye--

speaker: interviewee
text: Say 'bye-bye.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat Shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat Shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye-bye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for being here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you both for-- for putting up with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed very much being with you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
